commands.review_summary:
This PR adds Telegram direct-message ACP binding matching, a shared direct-peer binding helper exposed through `plugin-sdk/direct-peer-binding`, tests, package export metadata, and docs.

PR surface: Source +235, Tests +348, Docs +20, Config +4, Other +1. Total +608 across 15 files.

Reproducibility: yes. by source inspection: current main accepts direct/dm peer kinds in config types but Telegram ACP binding compilation and matching only handle topic/group-shaped ids. I did not run tests because this review is read-only.
commands.review_followup: - [P2] The remaining blocker is maintainer judgment on SDK public API scope, upgrade behavior, and which open PR should become the canonical fix for the related issue.

target.title: feat(channels/acp): support ACP bindings for direct-message peers
target.body:
## Motivation

OpenClaw can route a conversation to an **ACP backend** — an external agent runtime (the `acpx` agents: claude-code, codex, gemini, droid, opencode, or any custom ACP runtime) — via a `type:"acp"` binding.

On Telegram, a `type:"acp"` binding currently can't target a **1:1 direct message**: the matcher requires the bound conversation id to carry a group (negative) chat id, so a direct message's positive peer id is rejected and the turn falls through to the default model lane. The binding schema already allows `match.peer.kind: "direct"` (and the legacy `dm` alias) — this PR implements the runtime side so direct-peer bindings work.

A 1:1 DM is a natural way to reach a personal agent, and direct-peer bindings are the missing case here.

## Where this is useful

- **Personal assistant over DM** — DM the bot and have an ACP agent (codex / claude-code / gemini / a custom runtime) handle the conversation, instead of the built-in model lane.
- **Per-peer routing** — bind a specific user's DM to a specific agent/backend.

## What this does

- Adds a small, channel-agnostic helper `src/channels/plugins/direct-peer-binding.ts`: `isDirectPeerBinding` (kind `direct`/legacy `dm`), `compileDirectPeerConversation` (compiles to the normalized peer id, no parent), `matchDirectPeerConversation` (matches parent-less inbound DMs; rejects anything carrying a parent). Channels inject id canonicalization via an optional `normalizePeerId` hook.
- Wires **Telegram** to use it for the direct-peer path, injecting Telegram's existing positive-int / `direct:<id>` normalization. The existing matcher path and behavior are unchanged.
- Generic unit tests for the helper.

## Real behavior proof

- Behavior or issue addressed: a `type:"acp"` binding for a 1:1 Telegram direct message was rejected by the binding matcher (a direct message's positive peer id did not match) and the turn fell through to the default model lane. This change routes direct-message peers to the ACP backend.
- Real environment tested: a real `openclaw` gateway running on an Ubuntu 26.04 VM, built from this branch, with a live Telegram bot account and an ACP backend, configured with a `match.peer.kind: "direct"` ACP binding in `mode: "persistent"`.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch: started the gateway with `openclaw gateway`, then sent the bot a 1:1 Telegram DM `ping`, then a follow-up DM in the same conversation.
- Evidence after fix: copied live console output / redacted runtime log from the running `openclaw` gateway for the DM turn — the inbound direct message matched the direct-peer binding and produced a full ACP turn `session.started -> message.sent -> tool.event (x5) -> agent.reply` with reply text `pong`. Redacted gateway log lines:
```text
[telegram] [main] starting provider (@<bot>)
[plugins] registered ACP backend "<acp-backend>"
inbound message telegram:<redacted-user-id> -> @<bot> (direct)
session.started -> message.sent -> tool.event (x5) -> agent.reply  (reply: "pong")
[telegram] outbound send ok chatId=<redacted-user-id> operation=sendMessage
```
- Observed result after fix: the 1:1 DM matched the `peer.kind:"direct"` binding and was routed by the `openclaw` gateway to the ACP backend (not the model lane); the bot replied `pong`, and a follow-up DM in the same conversation round-tripped with retained context (persistent mode). Group/topic routing was unaffected.
- What was not tested: a full CI run of the Telegram suite inside this fork environment; the Telegram binding unit tests and the new helper unit tests were run locally instead.

## Scope / notes

- **Additive and opt-in** — existing bindings are untouched; the direct-peer and existing matchers are mutually exclusive (a DM can't match an existing binding and vice-versa).
- **Follow-up:** wire the helper as a default in the shared binding compiler so *any* channel — not just Telegram — gets direct-peer bindings automatically (today a channel opts in as Telegram does here).
target.author: pablospe
